Chief Complaint:

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
1997-09-11 Colostomy takedown, mobilization of splenic flexure


History of Present Illness:
30M s/p Hartmann's for perforated diverticulitis, complicated by
ischemic stoma, sepsis, requiring exploratory laparotomy,
colostomy revision. Patient now presenting for elective
colostomy reversal. Case required extensive lysis of adhesions,
tedious dissection, mobilization of splenic flexure, and careful
exposure and mobilization of rectal stump. Patient admitted to
the SICU intubated overnight for observation.

Past Medical History:
PM: ETOH, perforated diverticulitis

PSH: OSH procedure:
1991-05-03: Exploratory laparotomy, sigmoid colectomy and
formation of Hartmann's pouch colostomy

Musc Medical Center operations:

colostomy
1991-09-12: Abdominal washout, liver biopsy, abdominal closure

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the hospital for colostomy
take-down.  During the operative course he required 9 liters of
fluid with a 200cc blood loss.  Because of this large volume
requirement, he was transported to the intensive care unit for
monitoring.  He remained intubated overnight. He was extubated
on POD #1 without difficulty. He was started on a dilaudid PCA
and standing intravenous tylenol for pain control. He had mild
nausea with scant emesis around his Lindsey-gastric tube which was
controlled with zofran. His vital signs and electrolytes were
closely monitored.  His lactate trended down into the normal
range by the afternoon of POD #1. He completed 24 hours of
perioperative ancef and flagyl. His vitals were stable, with
adequate urine output, and normalizing lab values. His
Lindsey-gastric tube was removed.

He was transferred to the surgical floor on POD #1.  On POD #3,
he was started on clear liquids with advancement to a regular
diet.  His dilaudid PCA was discontinued and he was started on
oral analgesia. Initially, his surgical pain was not well
controlled and he required a dose adjustment of his diluadid,
and since then, he has remained comfortable.  He has been out of
bed and ambulating.

His vital signs have remained stable and he has been afebrile.
He has been tolerating a regular diet.  His white blood cell
count has normalized.  He is preparing for discharge home with
instructions to follow up with the acute care team in 2 weeks
for staple removal.

Medications on Admission:
none

holdfor increased seation, resp. rate <12

3 hours Disp #*40 Tablet Refills:*0

Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
take-down of colostomy


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.


Discharge Instructions:


You were admitted to the hospital for take-down of the
colostomy.  Your opertative course was stable.  You are now on
oral pain medications and you are preparing for discharge home
with the following instruction:

 Please call your doctor or return to the emergency room if you
have any of the following:


 * You experience new chest pain, pressure, squeezing or
 tightness.

*  Increased abdominal pain, abdominal distention

* New or worsening cough or wheezing.

* If you are vomiting and cannot keep in fluids or your
 medications.

* You are getting dehydrated due to continued vomiting,
 diarrhea or other reasons. Signs of dehydration include dry
 mouth, rapid heartbeat or feeling dizzy or faint when standing.

* You see blood or dark/black material when you vomit or have a
 bowel movement.


* You have shaking chills, or a fever greater than 101.5 (F)
 degrees or 38(C) degrees.

* Any serious change in your symptoms, or any new symptoms that
 concern you.

* Please resume all regular home medications and take any new
meds
 as ordered.

Activity:
No heavy lifting of items 08-09 pounds for 6 weeks. You may
resume moderate exercise at your discretion, no abdominal
exercises.

Wound Care:
You may shower, no tub baths or swimming.

If there is clear drainage from your incisions, cover with
clean, dry gauze
